verify.ts loop structure: `for (let attempt = 0; attempt <= RETRY_DELAYS_MS.length; attempt++)` — the loop condition and index arithmetic are confusing. On final attempt (attempt === 3), `attempt - 1 === 2` accesses RETRY_DELAYS_MS[2]=10000. The `if (attempt < RETRY_DELAYS_MS.length) continue` in the non-error branch creates implicit fall-through to a final `return ok(unconfirmed)`. This is functional but the loop shape is non-obvious — requires careful reading to confirm correctness.

guards all follow the same shape: DEFAULT_STATE constant, typed state interfaces, one or two exported check/record functions delegating to checkAndAppend/appendEntry — highly consistent
pay.ts fetchWithValidatedRedirects handles redirect loop with re-pinning and header stripping — complex but single coherent responsibility (SSRF-safe redirect following)
discover-sources.ts BUNDLED_CATALOG_PATH uses import.meta.url with regex string replace to navigate from tools/ back to project root — fragile path construction that assumes fixed directory depth
design_coherence.issues_preventing_higher_score: scan.ts has a dead parameter (domain passed into fetchFeed but never used in filtering). discover-sources.ts uses a fragile import.meta.url-based path computation. These are discrete defects rather than pervasive design incoherence.

evidence: All public tool functions (publish, reply, scan, tip, react, verify, attest, pay, discoverSources, connect) return typed ToolResult<T> or throw — return type annotations are accurate for all paths
connect() documents `@throws {DemosError}` and throws consistently on auth failure, bad permissions, symlink — no silent null returns
disconnect() return type is void and side-effect (expire session) matches the name — contract is sound

checkAndRecordDedup/checkAndRecordWrite/checkAndRecordTip: `record: boolean = false` parameter correctly documents check-only vs check+record behavior. Name uses 'checkAndRecord' which accurately signals both capabilities
DemosSession.getBridge() throws `Error` (not DemosError) when bridge unavailable — inconsistent with toolkit error pattern but documented in JSDoc

session.getAuthToken() and session.getSigningHandle() are named 'get' but also call checkExpired() which can mark the session as expired (side effect). This is documented in JSDoc `@throws` comment but the getter name hides the potential mutation

verify() returns ok({ confirmed: false }) when all retries exhaust without finding the tx AND no error occurred — caller cannot distinguish 'not yet confirmed' from 'permanently not on chain' without additional context
scan() accepts opts.domain but the domain is never applied to filtering — function contract implies domain filtering works but it silently no-ops (cross-references design_coherence finding)
discoverSources() docstring says 'Session required for withToolWrapper integration — only sourceCatalogPath is used for logic' — honest about the session being passed but not for auth
pay() step comments (1-9) align precisely with the code flow — inline documentation matches implementation
withToolWrapper's `defaultErrorCode` parameter is documented as the code to use 'when an unhandled exception is thrown' — accurate, this is exactly how it is used in practice
SdkBridge interface method `getDemos()` documents `@throws {Error}` when allowRawSdk is false — this throw is clearly documented and consistently implemented

VALID_STATUSES in discover-sources.ts is flagged deprecated with 0 importers but normalizeStatus() still calls it — deprecated annotation is on the Set but normalizeStatus internally uses it. This is internal use, not an exported contract issue
contract_coherence.issues_preventing_higher_score: scan() domain parameter is accepted in contract but never applied — a concrete contract violation. session.getAuthToken()/getSigningHandle() are getter-named methods with an expiry side effect (checkExpired can set _expired=true) which is non-obvious from the name alone, though JSDoc does document it.

evidence: verify.ts: `throw new Error('unreachable')` after the for loop (line 62) — annotated with a comment explaining TypeScript requires it; the for loop always returns on its final iteration. This is intentional dead-code-for-type-completeness, not a logic defect.

backoff.ts: `lastResult!` non-null assertions on lines 50-54 after the loop — TypeScript cannot prove lastResult is set (because loop might not execute if MAX_RETRIES=0), but MAX_RETRIES is a module constant set to 3 so attempt=0 always runs. The ! is technically safe but potentially misleading.

connect.ts lines 229-232: `Object.keys(connectOpts).length > 0` condition — connectOpts only gets populated when `algorithm !== 'ed25519'`, so this check duplicates the condition above. Equivalent to checking `algorithm !== 'ed25519'` a second time.

tip-spend-cap.ts lines 61-63: linear scan to find most recent timestamp via `for/if` loop — equivalent to `Math.max(0, ...state.entries.map(e => e.timestamp))` but more verbose. Not incorrect, just indirect.
scan.ts fetchFeed() accepts `domain?: string` parameter but never references it in body — dead parameter (no filtering, no query param)

discover-sources.ts: VALID_STATUSES Set is annotated @deprecated but normalizeStatus() still calls it internally — the deprecated annotation is on the set referenced in the same file, misleading about its liveness. normalizeStatus itself is @deprecated (0 importers) but is called by loadCatalog() in the same file.

session.ts `expired` getter (lines 149-156): lazily sets `_expired = true` on first timeout detection. Subsequent calls check `_expired` first (fast path). This is correct lazy initialization, not redundant checking.

pay.ts safeReadBody (lines 242-250): nested try/catch with `await import()` inside a catch — `import('../guards/state-helpers.js')` is called inside a try block that also catches failures, returning null on any failure. The logic is correct but double-nested try makes the success/failure paths harder to follow.

publish.ts: `checkAndRecordWrite` called twice with same args in same function — once with `record=false` (pre-check) and once with `record=true` (post-commit). Between calls, if no concurrent writers exist, the second call always succeeds. This is correct two-phase behavior, not redundant.

state-helpers.ts checkAndAppend: `if (newEntry) { state.entries.push(newEntry) }` followed by unconditional `await store.set(...)` — state is always persisted even when no entry added (intentional: prune-only persist). Comment explains this. Not a logic defect.
connect.ts detectContainer(): reads 3 different /proc and /dockerenv files with independent try/catch blocks — each check is genuinely independent. No dead paths.
logic_clarity.issues_preventing_higher_score: connect.ts has a redundant condition check (Object.keys(connectOpts).length > 0 when connectOpts is only populated under algorithm !== 'ed25519'). scan.ts fetchFeed has a dead parameter. pay.ts safeReadBody has double-nested try/catch with async import inside catch that reduces readability without adding safety.

evidence: schemas.ts: bidirectional compile-time type sync assertions (Forward: z.output<Schema> extends Interface, Reverse: Interface extends z.input<Schema>) for all 10 tool input types — strong positive pattern preventing schema/interface drift

sdk-bridge.ts lines 301-308: 6 chained `(result as any)?.` expressions to extract txHash from SDK broadcast response — necessary because SDK returns polymorphic shapes but the `any` chain bypasses type safety for the whole txHash extraction block

sdk-bridge.ts line 150: `const rpc = demos as unknown as DemosRpcMethods` — single cast at factory level, then typed `rpc` used throughout. This is a controlled cast pattern: one cast site, then typed from there.

sdk-bridge.ts: DemosRpcMethods interface defines the minimal typed surface expected from demos SDK — this is an intentional typing shell that isolates SDK volatility. The `as any` chains in publishHivePost are separate from this.
connect.ts: `signingHandle: unknown` in WalletData interface — the signing handle is typed as unknown because it is parsed from JSON. This is appropriate at the parsing boundary.

session.ts: `(this as Record<symbol, unknown>)[AUTH_TOKEN]` pattern for symbol-keyed storage — TypeScript does not support symbol-indexed access on typed classes, so the cast is necessary. No unsafe cast escapes the class boundary.

discover-sources.ts lines 72-84: `entries.map((entry: Record<string, unknown>) => ...)` — each field access is explicitly cast (`entry.id as string`). Verbose but type-accurate: each field is narrowed before use.
discover-sources.ts line 73: `safeParse(raw) as Record<string, unknown>` — cast is necessary because safeParse returns `unknown`. Acceptable at parse boundaries.

backoff.ts lines 50-54: `lastResult!.error!.code` — non-null assertions after loop; logically safe (attempt=0 always runs, error is checked before reaching this point) but TypeScript cannot verify this statically.

tool-wrapper.ts line 46: `(e as import('../types.js').DemosError)` — cast inside error handling, but only after a runtime check `isDemosError` confirmed the shape. This is a type narrowing pattern, not an unsafe cast.

ScanOptions, DiscoverSourcesOptions intentionally omitted from compile-time sync assertions in schemas.ts because they use .optional() which introduces undefined in the type — comment explains the omission.
connect.ts: `connectOpts: Record<string, unknown>` for SDK options passed to connectWallet — no stronger type exists since SDK options are opaque. Appropriate use of Record.
sdk-bridge.ts `TxModule` interface types `store/confirm/broadcast` returning `Promise<unknown>` — SDK tx module returns are untyped in the SDK itself, so unknown is the honest type here.
No `@ts-ignore` or `// @ts-expect-error` comments found across toolkit files — type suppressions are not used to paper over problems
type_safety.issues_preventing_higher_score: sdk-bridge.ts publishHivePost uses 6 chained `(result as any)?.` expressions for txHash extraction — a typed interface for the broadcast response shape would eliminate these. The `as any` chains mean TypeScript provides no protection for the txHash extraction path, which is security-sensitive (on-chain tx confirmation). This is the most impactful type safety issue.

cross_module_architecture.dimension_character: The toolkit has strong internal architecture — clean layering (types -> guards -> session -> tools -> index), no cycles, hub module (state-helpers) is small and pure, and the sdk-bridge pattern correctly isolates external SDK volatility. The single structural defect is connect.ts dynamically importing src/lib/auth.js, crossing into the application lib layer for auth token caching and challenge-response auth — this is the residual stub from a prior fix that was documented as resolved but the underlying coupling remains.
cross_module_architecture.score_rationale: Architecture scores well on the strengths (no cycles, clean directed imports, isolation patterns) but the active cross-boundary import in connect.ts is a real defect that directly contradicts the documented framework-agnostic property. A score of 77 reflects a mostly-clean architecture with one persistent boundary violation that has mild transitive reach (toolkit -> app lib -> app sdk).

high_level_elegance.dimension_character: The toolkit structure is generally domain-aligned and coherent — a new engineer can map directories to responsibilities quickly. The index.ts public API surface is honest and matches the header documentation. Two residual issues reduce elegance: feed-parser.ts belongs in guards/ or a shared util layer rather than tools/, and discoverSources() still carries documentation claiming it doesn't need a session while the signature requires one.
high_level_elegance.score_rationale: Strong domain-aligned decomposition and trustworthy documentation earn a high baseline. The score stops at 84 because of the feed-parser placement (discoverable but surprising) and the persistent discoverSources session-requirement contradiction that was not actually resolved in code — the docstring was updated but the parameter wasn't removed or made optional.

convention_outlier.dimension_character: The toolkit codebase has strong convention consistency: uniform named exports, consistent kebab-case file naming, camelCase functions throughout, and a clear guard API pattern. Two outliers exist: pay-spend-cap.ts still exports the pre-atomic checkPaySpendCap/recordPayment pair as non-deprecated functions alongside the new reservePaySpend, and pay.ts has one inline dynamic import of safeParse that breaks the static-imports-at-top-level convention.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: The conventions are tightly followed with minimal drift. Score of 83 reflects strong overall consistency with two specific outliers: the pay-spend-cap guard API protocol inconsistency (separate check/record vs unified checkAndRecord* peers) and the single inline dynamic import in pay.ts. Neither is severe but both are visible departures from the established patterns.

error_consistency.dimension_character: The toolkit has a well-designed error architecture: typed DemosError codes, consistent ToolResult<T> envelope, and a centralized withToolWrapper catch layer. The main residual issues are error code accuracy — connectSdk() maps all internal exceptions to AUTH_FAILED regardless of whether the failure was authentication-related, and apiCall()'s error return loses type information by converting to a plain string. Both reduce diagnosability without causing incorrect behavior.
error_consistency.score_rationale: Strong structural consistency (unified envelope, typed codes, centralized catch) earns a high base score. Score stops at 79 due to the AUTH_FAILED misclassification in connectSdk() (a real semantic error) and apiCall() discarding error type on network failures. These are not severe but are persistent after prior fixes addressed the more egregious silent-swallow issues.

naming_quality.dimension_character: The toolkit has strong naming quality overall. Domain operation names are precise, guard function names communicate semantics clearly, and the checkAndRecord*/reservePaySpend vocabulary is consistent and intentional. The previously-flagged issues (getTxModule get-prefix confusion, backward-compat alias ambiguity, recordPublish mismatch) are confirmed resolved. Remaining naming quality concerns are minor: localProvenance is slightly ambiguous and connectSdk understates what it does.
naming_quality.score_rationale: 86 reflects excellent domain naming throughout the toolkit with only minor ambiguity issues. The prior naming defects were all resolved (getTxModule renamed, aliases deprecated, recordPublish name fixed). The remaining two observations are low-severity — most engineers would understand localProvenance from context, and connectSdk is common shorthand for the auth+connect flow.

abstraction_fitness.dimension_character: The toolkit's abstraction layer is generally well-earned: withToolWrapper, checkAndAppend, SdkBridge, and parseFeedPosts all deliver measurable value without ceremony. The primary tension is in DemosSession serving as a transparent property bag — 12 public fields read directly by 8 tool files — which works but means tools have direct coupling to the session's internal structure rather than going through behavior-bearing methods. The StateStore one-implementation interface is legitimate given its test-injection role.
abstraction_fitness.score_rationale: The codebase scores above average (80/100) because every major abstraction is genuinely used and most indirection has clear purpose. The score is held below 85 by: DemosSession as a wide property bag (tools reach into session.urlAllowlist, session.stateStore, session.walletAddress etc. directly), and the connect→session option forwarding being essentially a 1:1 pass-through of 14 fields with no transformation. These are real costs but not severe defects given the toolkit's nature as a thin orchestration layer.

dependency_health.dimension_character: The production dependency set is minimal and purposeful: 5 packages, each with clear direct usage in production code. No duplicate-purpose libraries, no circular cycles, no leak of dev tooling into production. The only concern is @anthropic-ai/sdk being a heavier dependency (~6MB Anthropic SDK) used exclusively outside src/toolkit/, which matters for future packaging but not for the current monorepo structure.
dependency_health.score_rationale: Score 87/100 — above average, not perfect. The dependency set is clean and well-curated with no duplication or phantom deps. Held below 90 by the @anthropic-ai/sdk weight asymmetry: if packages/core is ever published as a standalone npm package, it would inherit this heavy dep despite having zero direct toolkit imports from it. For the current private monorepo this is low severity.

low_level_elegance.dimension_character: Function internals are mostly clean and direct, with well-named steps and bounded state mutation. The main recurring tension is: (1) loop structures that are functional but non-obvious (verify.ts retry loop), (2) a 7-level fallback chain with repeated `as any` casts in sdk-bridge.ts publishHivePost, (3) dead destructuring variables in url-validator.ts, and (4) duplicated validation logic between checkPaySpendCap and reservePaySpend. These are real craft defects but not severe.
low_level_elegance.score_rationale: Score 77/100 — below the toolkit's otherwise high quality. The dead `c`/`d` variables in url-validator, the non-obvious verify loop structure, and the duplicated spend validation logic are all addressable with single-function edits. The fallback chain in publishHivePost is a legitimate SDK adaptation challenge but the `as any` casts within it reduce confidence. Prior fixes (magic numbers, dead code, stub cleanup) have been completed, raising the floor but these remaining issues prevent a higher score.

mid_level_elegance.dimension_character: Integration seams are mostly clean and consistent — the withToolWrapper pattern, guard return contracts, and Zod boundary validation all work well together. The main defects are: verify.ts silently capping confirmation at the 50-post feed window (an undocumented narrowing of the verification contract), and connect() using throw semantics vs ToolResult semantics used by every other tool (intentional but creates caller asymmetry). The prior feed-parser duplication fix is confirmed resolved.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: Score 78/100. The toolkit has strong seam discipline in most places, kept below 85 by: the verify() 50-post window silent failure (callers cannot distinguish 'not confirmed' from 'outside search window'), the connect() throw-vs-return asymmetry, and scan.ts using a throw-then-catch pattern instead of returning an error directly from fetchFeed. These are real seam defects, not cosmetic.

test_strategy.dimension_character: The toolkit has broad test coverage with dedicated files for all major modules, behavioral (not snapshot) assertions, proper mock injection via SdkBridge, and correct elimination of timing dependencies via mocked sleep. The test quality enforcement layer (globalSetup assertion scanner + PostToolUse hook) is a strong institutional guarantee. Gaps are: no regression test for scan()'s broken domain parameter, potential test interference from discover-sources.ts module-level catalogCache, and some tools (react, parts of scan) covered only via broad tools.test.ts rather than dedicated focused suites.
test_strategy.score_rationale: Score 79/100. Test discipline is above average — no snapshot overuse, no timing fragility, proper mock boundaries, strong security path coverage. Held below 85 by: broken scan() domain behavior having no regression test (a tested defect is worth more than an untested defect), module-level catalogCache in discover-sources that can cause cross-test pollution, and missing targeted tests for the connect() error paths (symlink rejection, mode-600 failure, container detection).

api_surface_coherence.dimension_character: The toolkit public API is highly coherent for its core 10 tools — uniform (session, opts) shape, consistent ToolResult<T> return types, and Zod validation at every entry point. Two structural exceptions exist: connect/disconnect use throw/void semantics (intentional, documented), and reply() bypasses withToolWrapper for its validation path, silently skipping session.touch() on input errors.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: The codebase scores well above average for API surface coherence — consistent shape, typed contracts, and documented exceptions. The reply() validation bypass (no withToolWrapper wrapper, latencyMs:0, no touch()) is a genuine defect that creates an observable behavior difference from all sibling tools. The guard-vs-tool abstraction gap in the public export surface is a minor coherence cost. Score 77 reflects a strong surface with two actionable deviations.

authorization_consistency.dimension_character: The toolkit applies URL validation and allowlist checks consistently to tools that accept user-supplied URLs (attest, pay, publish), but skips these checks for tools that make outbound API calls to configured endpoints (scan, verify, react, tip). This asymmetry is partially justified by the distinction between user-controlled URLs and SDK-bridge-routed API calls, but creates an inconsistent trust model that is not documented as a design decision.
authorization_consistency.score_rationale: The positive security foundations are solid: Symbol-based token storage, HTTPS enforcement, requirePayeeApproval default-true, SSRF blocklist on user URLs. The gap is that 4 of 10 tools (scan, verify, react, tip) make outbound network calls without the URL validation applied to their siblings — even if those calls go to a configured base URL, the pattern inconsistency reduces confidence and would surprise a security reviewer. Score 74 — good baseline, documented asymmetry is the primary drag.

ai_generated_debt.dimension_character: The codebase is largely free of LLM-hallmark debt. Comments explain constraints rather than restate code, domain terminology is consistent, and debug logging is disciplined. Two specific patterns introduce modest debt: the isDemosError check is duplicated inline in tool-wrapper.ts alongside the exported isDemosErrorLike function, and safeReadBody in pay.ts uses a dynamic import where a static import would suffice.
ai_generated_debt.score_rationale: Strong baseline — no entry/exit log spam, no pass-through wrappers, no boilerplate error clones across files. The two defects are isolated and fixable in single edits. Score 72 reflects a clean codebase with a couple of AI-authorship artifacts that survive code review.

incomplete_migration.dimension_character: The codebase has two active write-rate-limit implementations: the legacy src/lib/write-rate-limit.ts (sync, file-based, ledger-object) used by both CLI runners and platform barrel, and the new src/toolkit/guards/write-rate-limit.ts (async, StateStore-backed, timestamp-entry). The toolkit guards also carry 6 deprecated shim functions retained for backward compatibility. The migration is in-progress but has no documented timeline and the old system is not marked deprecated.
incomplete_migration.score_rationale: The dual write-rate-limit system is a genuine incomplete migration — not a design choice, since the new toolkit version was built to replace the old one. The deprecated guard shims are correctly annotated but their continued re-export in index.ts keeps them in the public API indefinitely. The CLI's continued use of the old file means the migration is blocked. Score 71 — one major incomplete migration with a clear old/new split, plus 6 deprecated-but-exported shims.

package_organization.dimension_character: The project has strong organization at the boundaries (src/toolkit/, src/actions/, src/reactive/, packages/core/) but a significantly overloaded src/lib/ directory holding 42 heterogeneous files with no sub-organization. The lib/ flat pile contains at least 6 identifiable concern clusters (auth/identity, LLM/AI, attestation/claims, sources/feeds, scoring/quality, infrastructure). This is the main navigation and ownership problem in the codebase.
package_organization.score_rationale: The toolkit subsystem is well-organized (25/25 for that area), but src/lib/ at 42 files with no subfolder structure fails the >10 files with mixed concerns threshold clearly. src/adapter-specs.ts misplaced at src root and test-quality-validator.ts in production lib/ are additional minor signals. Score 68 — good peripheral structure, significant core lib/ organization debt that affects discoverability for new contributors.

initialization_coupling.dimension_character: The toolkit codebase demonstrates strong awareness of initialization coupling concerns: SDK imports are uniformly lazy-loaded inside async functions, per-session state is encapsulated in DemosSession and closure-scoped bridge factories, and no I/O or network calls occur at module scope. Two minor deviations exist: DEFAULT_STATE_DIR in state-store.ts calls homedir() at module import time (environment-read coupling), and connect.ts holds a module-level `_isContainer` mutable singleton (though it is process-invariant by design and lazily populated).
initialization_coupling.score_rationale: The codebase scores high (88/100) because the primary anti-patterns — import-time I/O, network calls, and global SDK singletons — are all absent. The lazy import pattern for the heavy Demos SDK is consistently applied. The single concrete defect (homedir() at module scope in state-store.ts) is a minor but real coupling issue that creates test fragility when HOME is not the intended state dir. The _isContainer singleton is a design-intentional process-scoped cache, not a coupling bug, but adds a small amount of module-level mutable state.

design_coherence.dimension_character: The toolkit shows strong structural consistency in the guard layer (uniform state shape, lock patterns, check/record separation) and appropriate complexity in pay.ts (D402 protocol has inherent multi-step structure). Two concrete design defects stand out: scan.ts passes a domain parameter to fetchFeed() that is silently dropped (unfulfilled API contract at the function call level), and discover-sources.ts uses a regex-based path construction from import.meta.url that is fragile and tied to a specific directory depth assumption.
design_coherence.score_rationale: Score of 82 reflects a codebase where design decisions are mostly sound and consistent but contains two identifiable coherence failures — one dead parameter (scan domain filtering silently no-ops) and one fragile structural dependency (catalog path via regex on __filename). No functions are genuinely God objects; pay.ts is long but has a single coherent responsibility. The guard layer is a positive design exemplar.

contract_coherence.dimension_character: The toolkit maintains strong contract coherence overall: return types are accurate across all code paths, error throwing vs returning is documented and consistent, and withToolWrapper provides a uniform error-normalization contract. Two concrete defects exist: scan()'s domain option is accepted in the signature but silently dropped in implementation (unfulfilled contract), and session getter methods (getAuthToken, getSigningHandle) carry a checkExpired() side effect that marks the session as expired — a mutation hidden behind a getter name, though it is JSDoc-documented.
contract_coherence.score_rationale: Score of 83 reflects a codebase with correct return type discipline and consistent throw-vs-return patterns, but two real contract violations: a silently no-op parameter that callers reasonably trust to work (scan domain), and getter methods with hidden mutation side effects. These are discrete, fixable issues rather than pervasive pattern failures, landing the score in the good-but-not-excellent range.

logic_clarity.dimension_character: The toolkit demonstrates consistently sound control flow with a small number of isolated clarity issues. The most significant is a dead parameter in fetchFeed (domain is accepted but ignored), a redundant condition check in connectSdk, and a deeply nested catch-with-async-import in safeReadBody. The majority of complex flows (pay() step sequence, verify() retry loop, checkAndAppend atomic check) are cleanly structured with accurate intent.
logic_clarity.score_rationale: Score of 84 reflects good baseline clarity — no identical branches, no always-false conditions, no truly dead code — but with a handful of minor clarity failures: redundant condition, dead parameter, and overly nested error handling. These are isolated single-file fixes with no systemic pattern. The code reads correctly in all cases; the issues are clarity obstacles rather than logic bugs.

type_safety.dimension_character: The toolkit shows strong type discipline at the boundaries that matter most: Zod schemas with bidirectional compile-time assertions, single-point SDK casts with typed facades, and no ts-ignore suppressions. The significant weakness is sdk-bridge.ts publishHivePost's txHash extraction using 6 chained `(result as any)?.` expressions — the broadcast response shape is untyped, leaving a security-sensitive path without type protection. The rest of the `as unknown as Type` casting is controlled and isolated to genuine typing boundaries.
type_safety.score_rationale: Score of 72 reflects a codebase with strong type discipline in the contract/schema layer but meaningful weakness in the SDK response handling. The Zod bidirectional assertions are a best-practice positive, but the any-chain for txHash extraction in the most security-critical path (on-chain publish confirmation) is a real gap. Type safety is the weakest dimension per operational knowledge (71%), and the evidence confirms this pattern.

issues.suggestion: Split the outer catch to distinguish SDK connection failures (demos.connect/connectWallet — NETWORK_ERROR) from authentication failures (AUTH_FAILED). At minimum, change the outer catch to use NETWORK_ERROR for the demos.connect() call specifically.
evidence: connect.ts lines 245-251: catch (e) { throw demosError('AUTH_FAILED', `SDK connection failed: ${(e as Error).message}`, true) }
This outer catch wraps everything inside the try block — including wallet parse failures, network failures, and transaction failures — all as AUTH_FAILED
A network timeout on demos.connect(rpcUrl) would produce AUTH_FAILED with retryable=true — correct retryable flag but wrong code (should be NETWORK_ERROR)
An invalid mnemonic (12-24 words check at parse level passes, but SDK rejects) would produce AUTH_FAILED — this one is accurate
The auth-specific catch at lines 235-243 correctly returns AUTH_PENDING_TOKEN for auth failures; the outer catch should use NETWORK_ERROR for non-auth SDK failures

issues.summary: sdk-bridge.ts apiCall() converts network errors to plain string, losing error type information
issues.suggestion: Add an optional error field to ApiCallResult or return a structured error object when ok=false. At minimum, preserve the original error message in a consistent location rather than in data.
evidence: sdk-bridge.ts lines 258-261: catch (err) { const message = ...; console.warn(...); return { ok: false, status: 0, data: message } }
The ApiCallResult type has no error field — error information is stuffed into data as a string
Callers (scan.ts, verify.ts, react.ts, tip.ts) check result.ok and then throw/return error — they lose the original exception type
scan.ts line 64-65: if (!result.ok) { throw new Error(`Feed API returned ${result.status}`) } — status is always 0 on network failure, making error messages misleading

issues.summary: connectSdk() understates its responsibility — it loads wallet credentials, connects SDK, and authenticates, not just connects
issues.suggestion: Rename to initSessionSdk() or connectAndAuthenticateSdk() to signal that auth is included. This is a low-severity naming issue — functionality is correct, only the name understates scope.
evidence: connect.ts connectSdk() performs: lazy SDK import, demos.connect(rpcUrl), wallet mnemonic resolution, demos.connectWallet(), SuperColony auth (ensureAuth) — four distinct operations
Name implies only SDK connection; the full flow includes wallet loading and auth
Callers (only connect.ts itself) won't be confused, but the name reduces auditability — a reader scanning for 'auth flow' may overlook this function

issues.summary: DemosSession exposes 12 public fields read directly by tools, making it a transparent property bag rather than a behavior-bearing session object
issues.suggestion: Accept as a known design decision (toolkit is intentionally thin) OR gradually encapsulate: e.g., session.getGuardContext() → { stateStore, walletAddress } to reduce direct field coupling. Not urgent — the fields are stable and the pattern is consistent.
evidence: publish.ts reads: session.stateStore, session.walletAddress, session.urlAllowlist, session.allowInsecureUrls
pay.ts reads: session.stateStore, session.walletAddress, session.urlAllowlist, session.allowInsecureUrls, session.payPolicy
tip.ts reads: session.stateStore, session.walletAddress, session.tipPolicy
Tools access session fields directly in their bodies rather than calling session methods — any rename or restructuring of session fields ripples to all 8 tool files
DemosSession has only 4 behavioral methods (getAuthToken, getBridge, setAuthToken, touch, expire, expired) vs 12 public data fields

issues.summary: @anthropic-ai/sdk is a heavy production dep (~6MB) with zero direct imports in src/toolkit/ — burdens future toolkit packaging
issues.suggestion: When packages/core is published, move @anthropic-ai/sdk to a separate CLI/harness package or make it an optional peer dependency. For now, document that toolkit consumers will pull this dep transitively.
evidence: package.json lists @anthropic-ai/sdk as a production dependency at ^0.78.0
grep of src/toolkit/ shows zero direct imports from @anthropic-ai/sdk
packages/core/ re-exports from src/toolkit/ barrel — any consumer of @demos-agents/core inherits the full dependency graph including @anthropic-ai/sdk
The actual SDK usage is in src/lib/llm-provider.ts (CLI harness layer), which is outside the toolkit boundary

issues.summary: checkBlockedIpv4 destructures [a, b, c, d] but c and d are never used in any blocking condition
issues.suggestion: Either use `const [a, b] = parts;` if c/d are genuinely never needed, or add the missing range checks that were intended (e.g., 192.0.2.0/24 TEST-NET uses [c]).
evidence: Line 158: `const [a, b, c, d] = parts;`
The function body references a, b in conditions (lines 162-202) but c and d appear in no condition
The scanner flags: 'unused: Unused vars: c' and 'unused: Unused vars: d' for this file
Functionally harmless but signals the function is incomplete — no range check uses the 3rd or 4th octet

issues.summary: verify.ts retry loop structure uses off-by-one index arithmetic and implicit fall-through that requires careful reading to verify correctness
issues.suggestion: Refactor to an explicit `for (let attempt = 1; attempt <= MAX_ATTEMPTS; attempt++)` with `if (attempt > 1) await sleep(...)` and `if (attempt === MAX_ATTEMPTS) return ok(unconfirmed)` — eliminates off-by-one arithmetic and the unreachable throw.
evidence: Loop: `for (let attempt = 0; attempt <= RETRY_DELAYS_MS.length; attempt++)` (line 30)
Sleep called with `RETRY_DELAYS_MS[attempt - 1]` — requires knowing attempt starts at 1 for first sleep
Non-error path: `if (attempt < RETRY_DELAYS_MS.length) continue;` then falls through to `return ok(unconfirmed)` — the final-attempt detection is implicit
The unreachable throw at line 62 documents that the compiler cannot verify the loop always returns — evidence of the structure's non-obviousness

issues.summary: reservePaySpend duplicates the amount validation from checkPaySpendCap with identical logic and error messages
issues.suggestion: Extract `validatePayAmount(amount, policy)` returning DemosError | null, then call it from both functions.
evidence: checkPaySpendCap (lines 34-45): isFinite check + maxPerCall check with specific error messages
reservePaySpend (lines 98-110): identical isFinite check + maxPerCall check with identical error messages
The duplication means a policy limit change or error message update must be applied in two places

issues.summary: scan() accepts a domain filter option but fetchFeed() silently ignores it in the API path
issues.suggestion: Either append `&domain=${encodeURIComponent(domain)}` to the feed path when domain is provided, or remove the domain parameter from ScanOptions and fetchFeed if the feed API does not support it.
evidence: fetchFeed(session, limit, domain) signature accepts domain
API call: `/api/feed?limit=${limit}` — domain is not appended
Scanner flags: 'unused: Unused imports: domain' for this file
ScanOptions.domain is documented in types.ts but has zero effect on the feed query

issues.summary: verify() silently returns unconfirmed for transactions outside the 50-post feed window — callers cannot distinguish 'not confirmed' from 'outside search window'
issues.suggestion: Add a `searchExhausted?: boolean` field to VerifyResult (or a third 'unknown' state) and set it when the feed returns the maximum limit of posts without finding the txHash. Alternatively, document the limitation clearly in the verify() JSDoc so callers can handle the ambiguity.
evidence: checkConfirmation uses `/api/feed?limit=50` — only checks the 50 most recent posts
If a txHash belongs to a post older than the 50th entry, verified=false is returned even if the post IS confirmed on-chain
No indication in the return type or error code that the result is 'unknown due to window' vs 'confirmed not present'
VerifyResult has `confirmed: boolean` with no `searchExhausted: boolean` or `windowLimited: boolean` field
Previously noted as a resolved issue (limit was 20, now 50) but the fundamental window problem remains at 50

issues.summary: scan() uses a throw-then-catch seam in fetchFeed() where returning an error result directly would be cleaner
issues.suggestion: Refactor fetchFeed() to return ScanPost[] | null (or throw only for truly unexpected errors), and handle the null case directly in scan() with err(demosError(...)).
evidence: fetchFeed() throws `new Error('Feed API returned ${result.status}')` on API failure
scan() wraps fetchFeed() in try/catch to convert the throw to a DemosError NETWORK_ERROR
All other tools (attest, pay, verify) return err(...) at the boundary without the throw-then-catch indirection
The pattern makes the error flow less obvious: reader must trace the throw through the catch to understand failure handling

issues.summary: scan() silently drops the domain filter with no regression test — the broken behavior could be 'fixed' or changed without test failure
issues.suggestion: Add a test that passes domain: 'crypto' and asserts the API call path includes 'domain=crypto' (if intended), OR asserts the path does NOT contain domain (if intentionally unimplemented). Either way, the behavior should be pinned by a test.
evidence: scan() accepts domain via ScanOptions but fetchFeed() never appends it to the API path
scan-opportunities.test.ts covers identifyOpportunities thresholds but does not pass a domain option in any test
No test that verifies domain IS or IS NOT used — if domain filtering is intentionally unimplemented, this should be asserted (expect path to not contain 'domain')

issues.summary: discover-sources.ts has a module-level Map cache (catalogCache) that persists across test runs and can cause cross-test pollution
issues.suggestion: Add a `clearCatalogCache()` export (test-only) that clears the Map, and call it in test afterEach. Alternatively, move the cache to a closure-scoped parameter or class to enable injection.
evidence: Line 20: `const catalogCache = new Map<string, Source[]>();` — module-level singleton
Once populated in a test run, subsequent tests in the same process see cached results from previous test's catalog file
Tests using different sourceCatalogPath values may get stale data from a previous test's path entry
No cache-clearing mechanism exposed for testing

issues.summary: connect() critical error paths (symlink rejection, mode-600 failure, container detection fallback) lack dedicated targeted tests
issues.suggestion: Add tests that mock lstat() to return isSymbolicLink()=true (expect rejection), mock fd.stat() to return mode=0o644 (expect rejection in non-container), and mock /proc file reads for container detection paths.
evidence: connect.ts has 5 distinct early-exit paths: Zod validation, HTTPS enforcement, symlink rejection, mode-600 rejection, container detection fallback
The container detection logic (detectContainer()) reads 3 /proc files — has no test for the detection paths
The symlink rejection and mode-600 check are security-critical paths
connect.test.ts exists but its coverage of these specific paths needs verification

issues.summary: reply() validates input outside withToolWrapper — session.touch() not called and latencyMs is 0 on validation failure
issues.suggestion: Wrap reply()'s body in withToolWrapper, moving validateInput inside the wrapper fn. This aligns reply() with all sibling tools and ensures touch()/onToolCall fire consistently.
evidence: reply() calls validateInput() before delegating to publish(). On failure, returns err(inputError, { path: 'local', latencyMs: 0 }) with no session.touch() and no onToolCall notification.
Every other tool (publish, scan, tip, react, attest, verify, pay, discoverSources) wraps all logic inside withToolWrapper which handles touch(), timing, and onToolCall.

This means: (a) session inactivity timer is not reset on reply() input errors, (b) onToolCall observer never fires for reply() failures, (c) latencyMs is always 0 for validation errors instead of actual elapsed time.

issues.summary: scan/verify/react/tip make outbound API calls without SSRF validation applied to sibling tools (attest/pay/publish)
issues.suggestion: Add SSRF validation for the supercolonyApi base URL in SdkBridge at bridge construction time (once per session) rather than per-call. Document the trust model: 'user-supplied URLs are validated per-call; session-configured endpoints are validated at connect time.' This resolves the inconsistency without adding per-call overhead to scan/verify/react/tip.
evidence: scan.ts fetchFeed calls bridge.apiCall('/api/feed?limit=...') — no validateUrl(), no allowlist check.
verify.ts checkConfirmation calls bridge.apiCall('/api/feed?limit=50') — no URL validation.
react.ts calls bridge.apiCall('/api/react') — no URL validation.
tip.ts calls bridge.apiCall('/api/feed?limit=50') for recipient resolution — no URL validation.
In contrast, attest.ts, pay.ts, and publish.ts all call validateUrl() before any outbound request.
The supercolonyApi base URL is set at connect() time and not re-validated at call time — a SSRF mitigation could be centralized in the bridge's apiCall.

issues.summary: isDemosError inline check in tool-wrapper.ts catch block duplicates the exported isDemosErrorLike() in the same file
issues.suggestion: Replace the inline isDemosError check with isDemosErrorLike(e) — the function is defined in the same file and is already exported for use elsewhere.
evidence: Line 43: const isDemosError = e && typeof e === 'object' && 'code' in e && 'message' in e && 'retryable' in e;

Lines 58-63: export function isDemosErrorLike(error: unknown): error is ReturnType<typeof demosError> { return Boolean(error && typeof error === 'object' && 'code' in error && 'message' in error && 'retryable' in error); }
The two checks are identical in logic — the inline one could simply call isDemosErrorLike(e).

issues.summary: safeReadBody in pay.ts uses dynamic import for safeParse instead of static import
issues.suggestion: Add 'import { safeParse } from ../guards/state-helpers.js' as a static import at the top of pay.ts and call it directly in safeReadBody.
evidence: Lines 246-248: const { safeParse } = await import('../guards/state-helpers.js'); return safeParse(text);
safeParse is a pure synchronous utility — there is no reason for it to be dynamically imported. State-helpers is already statically imported by other toolkit files.
The dynamic import adds async overhead and a try-catch that swallows the parse failure silently.

issues.summary: Two active write-rate-limit implementations coexist: legacy src/lib/ (sync/file) and toolkit src/toolkit/guards/ (async/StateStore) — CLI runners still use old system
issues.suggestion: 1. Add @deprecated JSDoc to src/lib/write-rate-limit.ts pointing to src/toolkit/guards/write-rate-limit.ts. 2. Plan CLI migration: session-runner and event-runner need to be updated to use a DemosSession + checkAndRecordWrite pattern, or accept that the CLI uses the legacy system as a deliberate long-lived consumer. 3. Remove the legacy exports from platform/index.ts once CLI migration is complete.
evidence: cli/session-runner.ts line 80: import { loadWriteRateLedger, canPublish, recordPublish, saveWriteRateLedger } from '../src/lib/write-rate-limit.js'
cli/event-runner.ts line 30: same import from src/lib/write-rate-limit.js
platform/index.ts line 19: exports canPublish, recordPublish from src/lib/write-rate-limit.ts
src/lib/write-rate-limit.ts has no @deprecated annotation despite the toolkit version existing and being maintained.
The two systems store state differently (file-based JSON ledger vs StateStore key-value) so they cannot be swapped transparently.

issues.summary: 6 deprecated guard functions remain in public index.ts export surface without removal timeline
issues.suggestion: Add a target removal version/milestone comment to each deprecated shim (e.g., '// Remove in toolkit v2.0'). Consider moving them to a separate 'src/toolkit/compat.ts' file not included in the default barrel, making them opt-in rather than default-visible.
evidence: src/toolkit/index.ts exports: checkWriteRateLimit, recordWrite (deprecated), checkDedup, recordPublish (deprecated), checkTipSpendCap, recordTip (deprecated)
All are marked @deprecated in source with pointers to checkAndRecord* replacements
No test imports of deprecated functions found in production code paths (tests do test them for backward compat coverage)
Re-exporting deprecated symbols in the public barrel keeps them discoverable to new consumers who may not notice the deprecation annotation

issues.summary: src/lib/ has 42 mixed-concern files in a flat layout — exceeds cohesion threshold by 4x
issues.suggestion: Stage 1: Create sub-directories within src/lib/: attestation/ (attestation-planner, attestation-policy, claim-extraction), llm/ (llm-provider, llm-claim-config), sources/ (source-discovery, source-scanner, feed-filter), scoring/ (scoring, quality-score, response-validator), auth/ (auth, identity). Stage 2: Move test-quality-validator.ts to tests/ or a dedicated testing-utils/. Stage 3: Update imports across cli/ and src/ with: find src cli -name '*.ts' | xargs sed -i 's|from "../lib/attestation-|from "../lib/attestation/|g' (repeat per cluster). Validate with npx tsc --noEmit after each stage.
evidence: 42 files under src/lib/ with no subfolders (excluding sources/ subdirectory)

At least 6 concern clusters identifiable: (1) auth/identity: auth.ts, identity.ts; (2) LLM/AI: llm-provider.ts, llm-claim-config.ts; (3) attestation/claims: attestation-planner.ts, attestation-policy.ts, claim-extraction.ts; (4) sources/feeds: source-discovery.ts, source-scanner.ts, feed-filter.ts, signals.ts, signal-detection.ts; (5) scoring/quality: scoring.ts, quality-score.ts, response-validator.ts; (6) infrastructure: write-rate-limit.ts, budget-tracker.ts, tx-queue.ts, fetch-with-timeout.ts, subprocess.ts, log.ts, state.ts
test-quality-validator.ts is testing infrastructure co-located with production business logic

issues.summary: src/adapter-specs.ts is at the src/ root despite src/adapters/ subdirectory existing
issues.suggestion: Move src/adapter-specs.ts to src/adapters/specs.ts or src/adapters/adapter-specs.ts. Update imports with: grep -r 'adapter-specs' src cli --include='*.ts' to find all callers, then update paths.
evidence: src/adapters/ directory exists as a subdirectory of src/
src/adapter-specs.ts lives at src/ root — import affinity is clearly with the adapters directory

issues.summary: DEFAULT_STATE_DIR reads homedir() at module import time in state-store.ts
issues.suggestion: Move the DEFAULT_STATE_DIR computation inside the constructor: `this.dir = dir ?? resolve(homedir(), '.config', 'demos');`. This defers the homedir() call to construction time, allowing test code to override HOME between imports and construction without requiring module re-evaluation.
evidence: Line 17: `const DEFAULT_STATE_DIR = resolve(homedir(), '.config', 'demos');` — this executes at import time
homedir() reads the HOME environment variable; tests that want to override HOME must do so before any import of state-store.ts
Tests that pass an explicit dir to FileStateStore constructor are unaffected, but tests relying on the default are coupled to the process HOME at import time

issues.summary: scan() accepts domain filter option but fetchFeed() ignores it — filtering is never applied
issues.suggestion: Either append domain as a query parameter if the feed API supports it (`/api/feed?limit=${limit}&domain=${encodeURIComponent(domain)}`), or apply client-side filtering after parseFeedPosts: `const filtered = domain ? posts.filter(p => p.tags?.includes(domain) || p.category === domain) : posts`. Remove domain from fetchFeed signature if filtering is done post-parse. Document which approach is intentional.
evidence: scan() line 32: `const posts = await fetchFeed(session, limit, opts?.domain);` — domain is passed
fetchFeed() signature (line 59): `async function fetchFeed(session: DemosSession, limit: number, domain?: string)` — domain accepted but never used
fetchFeed builds path `/api/feed?limit=${limit}` with no domain query param
identifyOpportunities() receives all posts unfiltered — no post-fetch domain filtering either
User calling `scan(session, { domain: 'crypto' })` receives all posts, not domain-filtered posts

issues.summary: BUNDLED_CATALOG_PATH uses regex string replace on import.meta.url — fragile, assumes fixed directory depth
issues.suggestion: Replace the regex-based path with `resolve(fileURLToPath(new URL('../../../config/sources/catalog.json', import.meta.url)))`. This is the standard ESM pattern for resolving sibling files relative to the current module and is robust to imports from different working directories.
evidence: Lines 23-28: `fileURLToPath(import.meta.url).replace(/\/src\/toolkit\/tools\/.*$/, '')` — navigates up by stripping a hardcoded path suffix
If discover-sources.ts ever moves (e.g., during PR2-5 file migration), the regex silently fails to strip the suffix, and the resolved path points to a non-existent directory
The regex matches the literal string `/src/toolkit/tools/` — renaming the tools/ directory would silently break catalog loading at runtime
Alternative: use `new URL('../../../config/sources/catalog.json', import.meta.url)` which uses relative path navigation — explicit, move-safe, and standard ESM idiom

issues.reasoning: sdk-bridge.ts apiCall catch block returns a default error object `{ ok: false, status: 0, data: message }` rather than throwing. This is intentional — the SdkBridge interface documents apiCall as returning ApiCallResult, never throwing. The catch-and-return pattern is the correct design for a bridge that needs to normalize network failures into a typed result. The console.warn preserves observability. Not a design defect.

Magic numbers flagged across 6 guard files are all named constants: DAILY_LIMIT, HOURLY_LIMIT, HOUR_MS in write-rate-limit.ts; DAY_MS and GUARD_LOCK_TTL_MS exported from state-helpers.ts and shared; INACTIVITY_TIMEOUT_MS in session.ts with explanatory comment. The scanner likely fired on numeric literal computations like `30 * 60 * 1000` — these are named constant initializations, not embedded magic numbers in logic. No systemic fix needed.

High cyclomatic complexity across 7 files is domain-appropriate: url-validator.ts checks 9 IP ranges (inherently branchy), pay.ts implements a 9-step D402 protocol (sequential steps with independent error paths), connect.ts handles 3 wallet format variants, feed-parser.ts normalizes multiple API response shapes. None of these represent mixed concerns — they are single-responsibility functions with genuine conditional complexity. A lint rule to cap complexity would produce false positives here.

issues.summary: scan() accepts opts.domain in its contract but never applies the filter — silently no-ops
issues.suggestion: Either implement domain filtering (post-parse: `const filtered = domain ? posts.filter(p => p.category === domain || p.tags?.includes(domain)) : posts`) or remove the domain parameter from ScanOptions and ScanOptionsSchema until it is implemented. Returning unfiltered results while accepting a filter parameter is a contract lie.
evidence: ScanOptions type defines `domain?: string` as an accepted parameter
scan() passes opts?.domain to fetchFeed() which accepts but ignores it — no query param, no post-filter
Callers calling scan(session, { domain: 'crypto' }) receive ALL posts, not domain-filtered results
The function's API contract (parameter exists → filter applies) is violated silently with no error or warning

issues.summary: getAuthToken() and getSigningHandle() are getter-named but call checkExpired() which mutates _expired state
issues.suggestion: Rename to `requireAuthToken()` and `requireSigningHandle()` to signal that these methods enforce a precondition and may throw. Alternatively, keep getter names but move expiry-marking into touch() and expire() only, making the `expired` getter truly read-only by not setting `_expired` in the getter path (instead letting the inactivity check return true without persisting). The JSDoc acknowledgment reduces the severity but does not remove the contract confusion.
evidence: getAuthToken() line 113-116: calls this.checkExpired() before returning token
checkExpired() at line 166-170: calls `this.expired` getter which can set `this._expired = true` on first detection of timeout
Getter name convention implies read-only access — mutation of `_expired` is unexpected
JSDoc on getAuthToken documents `@throws {Error} if session is expired. Side effect: marks session as expired on first check` — side effect is acknowledged but name is still misleading

issues.summary: connectSdk() re-checks `Object.keys(connectOpts).length > 0` to decide which connectWallet overload to call, but connectOpts is only populated when `algorithm !== 'ed25519'`
issues.suggestion: Simplify to: `const address = algorithm !== 'ed25519' ? await demos.connectWallet(mnemonic, { algorithm }) : await demos.connectWallet(mnemonic);`. This eliminates the intermediate object and the indirect condition.
evidence: Line 224-226: `const connectOpts: Record<string, unknown> = {}; if (algorithm !== 'ed25519') { connectOpts.algorithm = algorithm; }`
Line 229-231: `const address = Object.keys(connectOpts).length > 0 ? await demos.connectWallet(mnemonic, connectOpts) : await demos.connectWallet(mnemonic);`
The length check is equivalent to `algorithm !== 'ed25519'` — it cannot be true when connectOpts is empty, and connectOpts is only populated in one branch
A reader must trace back through connectOpts construction to understand what the length check actually guards

issues.summary: safeReadBody() uses double-nested try/catch with async import inside the inner catch — success/failure paths are hard to follow
issues.suggestion: Add `import { safeParse } from '../guards/state-helpers.js'` as a static import at the top of pay.ts, then simplify safeReadBody: `async function safeReadBody(response: Response): Promise<unknown> { try { const text = await response.text(); try { return safeParse(text); } catch { return text; } } catch { return null; } }`. This eliminates the dynamic import and makes the control flow clearer.
evidence: Lines 242-250: outer try reads response.text(), inner try safeParse(text), catch returns text, outer catch returns null

`await import('../guards/state-helpers.js')` is called inside the inner try on every safeReadBody call — this is a dynamic import for a module that is always available, adding unnecessary latency and complexity
safeParse is already available as a static import in other files — could be imported statically at the top of pay.ts

issues.summary: publishHivePost txHash extraction uses 6 chained `(result as any)?.` expressions — no type protection on on-chain confirmation path
issues.suggestion: Define a local interface for the known SDK broadcast response shapes: `interface BroadcastResponse { response?: { data?: { transaction?: { hash?: string } }; results?: Record<string, { hash?: string }> }; hash?: string; txHash?: string; }`. Then cast `result as BroadcastResponse` once and access typed properties. This provides TypeScript validation of property names without requiring changes to the upstream SDK types.
evidence: Lines 301-308: `const confirmHash = (validity as any)?.response?.data?.transaction?.hash;` + 5 more `(result as any)?.` chains

txHash is used as the return value of publishHivePost — if the SDK changes response shape, TypeScript provides no warning, and the function falls through to `throw new Error('HIVE post broadcast succeeded but txHash not found')`
The pattern `(x as any)?.a?.b?.c` is equivalent to unchecked JavaScript — TypeScript gives zero type errors even if the property names are wrong
The broadcast response is typed as `unknown` from TxModule.broadcast() — this is honest, but the extraction should use typed guards or a local interface for known shapes

issues.summary: backoff.ts uses non-null assertions (!) on lastResult and lastResult.error after loop — TypeScript cannot verify safety
issues.suggestion: Add a type-safe check before the exhaustion return: `if (!lastResult || lastResult.ok || !lastResult.error) { return err(demosError('NETWORK_ERROR', 'Retry loop exhausted without error', false), { path: 'local', latencyMs: 0 }); }`. This eliminates non-null assertions and makes the control flow verifiable without relying on implicit knowledge of MAX_RETRIES.
evidence: Lines 50-54: `lastResult!.error!.code` and `lastResult!.error!.message` and `lastResult!.provenance`

lastResult is `ToolResult<T> | undefined` — the undefined case is reachable if loop never executes (MAX_RETRIES=0 would mean attempt 0 runs once, but the ! signals the developer cannot prove this to TypeScript)
The retry loop always runs attempt=0, so lastResult IS always set, but TypeScript cannot prove it from the type alone

The error assertion `lastResult!.error!` is also unsafe: a ToolResult can have `ok: true` with no error — this branch is only reached when the last result had a retryable error, but the type doesn't encode that

add.header: Auth boundary cross-layer import in connect.ts
add.description: connect.ts dynamically imports src/lib/auth.js for ensureAuth and loadAuthCache, crossing from the toolkit's documented framework-agnostic layer into the application lib layer. This is the main remaining boundary violation after prior fixes.

add.header: Toolkit internal layering is clean
add.description: All toolkit-internal imports follow correct direction: tools depend on types/session/guards/schemas, guards depend on types/state-helpers, no cycles. The single boundary violation is the outbound auth import.

add.header: Index.ts public surface matches stated API
add.description: The index.ts header comment lists the exact same API surface that index.ts actually exports — documentation and code are in sync at the package entry point.

add.header: discoverSources session-requirement contradiction persists
add.description: The prior fix updated the docstring but left the DemosSession parameter as required. The function comment now reads 'only sourceCatalogPath is used for logic' while the parameter is still required for withToolWrapper integration — a cleaner fix would make session optional.

add.header: Guard deprecation protocol is consistent
add.description: All guards that have deprecated functions mark them with @deprecated JSDoc. The pattern is clean and consistent across write-rate-limit, tip-spend-cap, and dedup-guard.

add.header: pay-spend-cap primary API not unified
add.description: pay-spend-cap.ts was not updated to the checkAndRecord* pattern when other guards were unified. checkPaySpendCap and recordPayment remain as primary (non-deprecated) exports, unlike peer guards which deprecated their split forms.

add.header: ToolResult envelope is consistent across all tools
add.description: Every tool except connect/disconnect returns ToolResult<T> with typed DemosError. The withToolWrapper centralized catch prevents raw Error leakage. This is a strong positive pattern.

add.header: connectSdk catch over-broad error code mapping
add.description: The outer try/catch in connectSdk() maps all exceptions to AUTH_FAILED. This was appropriate when the function only called auth operations, but the scope grew to include SDK connection and wallet loading.

add.header: checkAndRecord* guard naming pattern is settled
add.description: The atomic check+record guard functions use a consistent checkAndRecord* prefix across write-rate-limit, dedup-guard, and tip-spend-cap. This naming communicates single-lock atomic semantics clearly and is now the established convention.

add.header: Domain operation names are precise throughout
add.description: Tool function names (publish, reply, react, tip, scan, verify, attest, pay, discoverSources) map directly to network operations with no generic verbs in the public API surface.

add.header: StateStore interface earns its existence via test injection
add.description: FileStateStore is the only production implementation, but test suites universally inject mock StateStores. The interface's value is substitutability for testing, not runtime polymorphism.

add.header: withToolWrapper is a well-earned combinator abstraction
add.description: All 8 tool files use withToolWrapper to eliminate timing/touch/telemetry/error-wrap boilerplate. Zero ceremony — every call site gains concrete value.

add.header: DemosSession is a transparent property bag by design
add.description: Session exposes 12 public fields read directly by tools rather than encapsulating behavior. This is a deliberate thin-orchestration design choice — coherent but creates field-coupling across all tools.

add.header: Production dependency set is minimal and purposeful
add.description: All 5 production dependencies (anthropic sdk, demosdk, proper-lockfile, yaml, zod) have clear direct usage. No phantom, duplicate, or over-engineered dependencies.

add.header: @anthropic-ai/sdk weight asymmetry in toolkit packaging
add.description: SDK is used only in src/lib/llm-provider.ts (CLI layer), not in src/toolkit/. When packages/core is published as a standalone package it will unnecessarily inherit this ~6MB dep.

add.header: pay.ts pipeline steps are clean and well-delineated
add.description: 9-step payment pipeline uses comment headers and named locals to make each step's purpose clear. Extraction level is balanced — no monolith, no micro-fragmentation.

add.header: url-validator checkBlockedIpv4 has dead destructure variables
add.description: c and d are destructured but never referenced in blocking conditions, flagged by scanner. Either incomplete range coverage or unnecessary destructuring.

add.header: scan() domain parameter is silently discarded
add.description: ScanOptions.domain is accepted and passed to fetchFeed() but never appended to the API query path. The filter appears to work but has no effect.

add.header: withToolWrapper is the canonical seam pattern — consistently applied
add.description: All 8 tool files use withToolWrapper with identical (session, toolName, errorCode, fn) signature. The seam design eliminates boilerplate and enforces consistent error wrapping at boundaries.

add.header: Guard modules return consistent DemosError | null contracts
add.description: All 5 guard modules use DemosError | null return, enabling uniform guard invocation across all tools. The check-only vs check-and-record API is consistent.

add.header: verify() 50-post feed window silently narrows verification contract
add.description: checkConfirmation queries /api/feed?limit=50 — any transaction outside the 50-most-recent posts returns confirmed=false with no indication the search was window-limited. Callers cannot distinguish confirmed-absent from window-exceeded.

add.header: No snapshot tests — all assertions are behavioral
add.description: Zero snapshot-based tests in the toolkit test suite. All assertions check specific values, error codes, and function contracts. Test suite is resilient to implementation refactoring.

add.header: Test quality enforcement layer is institutional
add.description: Vitest globalSetup scans all test files for assertion-free tests before running. PostToolUse hook warns on write. The anti-vibe-testing culture is mechanically enforced, not just convention.

add.header: security-critical paths have dedicated security-hardening tests
add.description: tests/toolkit/security-hardening.test.ts covers SSRF validation, allowlist enforcement, AUTH_PENDING_TOKEN — security paths are treated as first-class test targets.

add.header: discover-sources catalogCache causes cross-test pollution risk
add.description: Module-level Map cache is never cleared between tests. Tests using different catalog paths in the same process may get stale results from a previous test's cache entry.

add.header: Uniform (session, opts) shape across all 10 tools
add.description: Every tool follows the same parameter ordering and return type. This consistency is a deliberate design constraint enforced by the shared withToolWrapper and ToolResult<T> types.

add.header: connect/disconnect exception is intentional and documented
add.description: connect() throws DemosError and disconnect() returns void — these deviate from ToolResult by design because they manage session lifecycle rather than tool operations. The @throws JSDoc annotation documents this contract explicitly.

add.header: reply() bypasses withToolWrapper on validation failure
add.description: reply() validates before delegating to publish() rather than wrapping all logic in withToolWrapper. This silently breaks session.touch(), onToolCall notification, and latencyMs accuracy for validation error paths.

add.header: User-supplied URLs validated; bridge API calls not validated
add.description: The trust model splits: tools accepting user URLs (attest, pay, publish) apply SSRF validation per-call. Tools using the session-configured supercolonyApi endpoint (scan, verify, react, tip) do not. This is partially justified but undocumented.

add.header: requirePayeeApproval defaults to true — strong security default
add.description: pay() requires explicit trustedPayees or requirePayeeApproval:false to send funds. This is a secure-by-default configuration that prevents accidental payments to untrusted recipients.

add.header: Comment discipline is strong — WHY not WHAT
add.description: Security rationale comments (container chmod, DNS rebinding, D402 flow) explain non-obvious constraints. No code-restating comments found in production paths.

add.header: No entry/exit debug logging or full object dumps
add.description: Production code uses console.warn only for genuine operator-relevant warnings (auth fallback, state corruption, untrusted feed resolution). No function lifecycle logging.

add.header: Dual write-rate-limit systems: CLI uses legacy, toolkit uses new
add.description: cli/session-runner.ts and cli/event-runner.ts still import from src/lib/write-rate-limit.ts (sync, file-based). The toolkit's async StateStore version exists but hasn't replaced the CLI's usage. This is a genuine incomplete migration, not a design choice.

add.header: Deprecated guard shims are annotated but remain in public API
add.description: 6 deprecated functions in 3 guard files are @deprecated-annotated and re-exported from index.ts. No removal timeline is set. The annotations are correct; the gap is that they remain first-class exports.

add.header: src/toolkit/ organization is a positive reference pattern
add.description: The toolkit subsystem (session, types, schemas, tools/, guards/) demonstrates clear role-based organization under 10 files per folder. This is the model to apply to src/lib/ reorganization.

add.header: src/lib/ is a 42-file flat catch-all with 6 concern clusters
add.description: The lib/ directory accumulated all shared utilities without subfolder organization. Six identifiable clusters (auth/identity, LLM, attestation/claims, sources/feeds, scoring/quality, infrastructure) are intermixed in a flat layout that impedes navigation and ownership clarity.

add.header: Lazy SDK import pattern consistently applied
add.description: All heavy SDK imports (Demos, DemosTransactions, D402Client, auth module) are deferred inside async functions rather than at module scope. This is a deliberate, consistent pattern that prevents import-time side effects from crypto polyfills and global state mutation that the SDK may perform on load.

add.header: Closure-scoped bridge caches prevent cross-session state pollution
add.description: sdk-bridge.ts uses closure-local variables (cachedTxModule, cachedD402Client) for lazy loading within each bridge instance. Each session gets an isolated bridge factory, so there is no shared mutable module-level cache that could cause ordering dependencies between sessions.

add.header: homedir() read at module scope in state-store.ts
add.description: DEFAULT_STATE_DIR is computed once at import time via homedir(). Tests relying on the default state dir are coupled to the HOME env var value at import time rather than at construction time. Minor fragility that can be fixed by moving the computation into the constructor.

add.header: Guard layer is design exemplar — consistent shape
add.description: All 6 guards follow identical structure: typed state interface, DEFAULT_STATE constant, delegating to checkAndAppend/appendEntry from state-helpers. This consistency means adding a new guard requires minimal cognitive overhead and reduces the risk of divergent patterns.

add.header: pay.ts complexity is protocol-inherent not design smell
add.description: pay() is 217 lines because D402 has 9 sequential protocol steps each with distinct failure modes. Splitting the function would create artificial seams across a single atomic transaction. The numbered step comments make the flow navigable.

add.header: scan domain filter is a dead parameter
add.description: scan() accepts opts.domain but fetchFeed() never applies it — neither as a query parameter nor post-parse filter. This is an unfulfilled API promise that silently no-ops for any caller relying on domain filtering.

add.header: Return type and throw contracts accurate throughout
add.description: Every public tool function returns ToolResult<T> with accurate types across all code paths. connect() consistently throws DemosError on all failure paths. No undocumented null returns from functions that declare non-nullable return types.

add.header: checkAndRecord* naming accurately signals dual behavior
add.description: The checkAndRecord* naming convention (checkAndRecordTip, checkAndRecordWrite, checkAndRecordDedup) with a boolean `record` parameter is an honest contract — the name signals both operations are available and the parameter controls which occurs. This avoids hidden side effects.

add.header: scan domain filter is unfulfilled contract promise
add.description: ScanOptions.domain is accepted by scan() but never applied — neither as a query param nor post-parse filter. Any caller relying on domain filtering gets silently unfiltered results. The parameter is a contract lie.

add.header: verify.ts unreachable-throw is intentional type annotation
add.description: The `throw new Error('unreachable')` after the retry loop exists because TypeScript cannot prove the loop always returns. It is annotated with an explanatory comment and is not a real dead-code defect.

add.header: Pay/publish two-phase check+record is correct, not redundant
add.description: publish() and tip() call check functions twice: once with record=false (pre-flight) and once with record=true (post-commit). The pattern is intentional — mutations are recorded only after the chain operation succeeds. The two calls serve different purposes.

add.header: Zod bidirectional compile-time assertions are best practice
add.description: schemas.ts uses both forward (schema output extends interface) and reverse (interface extends schema input) type assertions to prevent drift. This is a proactive type safety measure that catches missing required fields and schema/interface divergence at compile time.

add.header: Single-point SDK cast with typed facade is controlled
add.description: sdk-bridge.ts casts demos to DemosRpcMethods once at factory scope, then uses the typed `rpc` variable throughout the bridge. This isolates the cast to one location and provides type safety for all subsequent SDK method calls.

add.header: publishHivePost txHash extraction is untyped any-chain
add.description: The on-chain publish confirmation path uses 6 `(result as any)?.` chains to extract txHash from the SDK broadcast response. No TypeScript protection exists for this security-sensitive path. A local interface for known response shapes would address this.
